paper_title: Beyond Alignment: Value Diversity as a Collective Property in Multicultural Agent Systems

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Multicultural multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed in globally diverse settings, where different agents are grounded in different cultural backgrounds. Existing cultural evaluation focuses on value alignment: how closely a single agent matches a target culture. Yet alignment is a per-agent property and cannot reveal whether a system, taken as a whole, preserves the cultural plurality it is meant to represent. We propose value diversity as a system-level evaluation axis for multicultural agent systems, defined through the dissimilarity between culturally conditioned agents' responses on a shared value survey. Using the World Values Survey, we evaluate 19 cultures and 18 backbone models across a wide range of system configurations. We find that diversity is largely uncorrelated with alignment, indicating that the two capture complementary system properties, and that current multicultural agent systems fall substantially below human societies in value diversity. Mixed-backbone systems narrow this gap but do not close it, and the gap persists across culture compositions and agent scales. Social interaction further erodes diversity by driving agents toward consensus, and a participatory budgeting case study shows that this homogenization narrows the breadth of collective decision-making. Together, our results establish value diversity as a distinct evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems and reveal a persistent homogenization tendency in current LLM-based societies. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github.com/iNLP-Lab/MultiAgent-Diversity.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 4 ] . [ 1 5 8 9 5 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r Beyond Alignment: Value Diversity as Collective Property in Multicultural Agent Systems Shaoyang Xu1 Jingshen Zhang Long P. Hoang1 Jinyuan Li2 Wenxuan Zhang1* 1 Singapore University of Technology and Design 2 Washington University in St. Louis

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Multicultural multi-agent systems are increasingly deployed in globally diverse settings, where different agents are grounded in different cultural backgrounds. Existing cultural evaluation focuses on value alignment: how closely single agent matches target culture. Yet alignment is per-agent property and cannot reveal whether system, taken as whole, preserves the cultural plurality it is meant to represent. We propose value diversity as systemlevel evaluation axis for multicultural agent systems, defined through the dissimilarity between culturally conditioned agents responses on shared value survey. Using the World Values Survey, we evaluate 19 cultures and 18 backbone models across wide range of system configurations. We find that diversity is largely uncorrelated with alignment, indicating that the two capture complementary system properties, and that current multicultural agent systems fall substantially below human societies in value diversity. Mixed-backbone systems narrow this gap but do not close it, and the gap persists across culture compositions and agent scales. Social interaction further erodes diversity by driving agents toward consensus, and participatory budgeting case study shows that this homogenization narrows the breadth of collective decision-making. Together, our results establish value diversity as distinct evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems and reveal persistent homogenization tendency in current LLM-based societies. Our code and data are publicly available at https://github. com/iNLP-Lab/MultiAgent-Diversity.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: The growing capability of large language models (LLMs) has spawned rapidly expanding ecosystem of LLM-based agents (Wang et al., 2024a; Luo et al., 2025), and multi-agent systems built *Corresponding Author 1 from them are emerging as major research direction (Guo et al., 2024; Tran et al., 2025). Early work focused on improving task capability (Du et al., 2024; Liang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025); more recent efforts have moved toward agentonly social platforms in which no human participates (Feng et al., 2026; Manik and Wang, 2026; Lin et al., 2026; Jiang et al., 2026; Moltbook, 2026). Existing work on the cultural dimension of LLMs has largely focused on value alignment: an agent answers items from social-value survey, and its responses are compared against those of real population (Cao et al., 2023; AlKhamissi et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024b; Masoud et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2025; Ki et al., 2025). This paradigm evaluates how well single agent represents its assigned culture. However, in multicultural multi-agent systems, property of the whole system becomes more important: whether culturally grounded agents collectively maintain heterogeneous values when they coexist and interact (Park et al., 2023; Frisch and Giulianelli, 2024; Huang et al., 2025; Manik and Wang, 2026; Moltbook, 2026). Alignment alone is insufficient because even strongly aligned agents may still collapse toward homogeneous value space. We therefore argue that multicultural agent systems require distinct system-level evaluation axis, which we call value diversity. Defining system-level value diversity matters for two reasons. First, as collective property of systems intended to represent or serve human societies, diversity naturally connects to the broader goal of pluralistic alignment (Kekes, 1996; Conitzer et al., 2024; Sorensen et al., 2024). Second, diversity has recently emerged as an important driver of collective decision-making and collaborative reasoning in LLM systems (Du et al., 2024; Kim et al., 2026; Pappu et al., 2026). Recent work has also begun to examine diversity dynamics in multi-agent interactions (Huang et al., 2025; Han et al., 2026; Yang et al., 2026; Zhu et al., 2026). However, the degree of diversity exhibited by an agent system remains poorly defined. Our work targets multicultural agent systems and addresses this gap from the perspective of cultural values. Our framework extends cultural evaluation from the individual-agent level to the system level. Given value survey and multicultural agent system, each agent answers survey questions conditioned on its assigned cultural identity. Value alignment measures the similarity between an agents responses and its corresponding cultural reference, capturing local cultural fidelity. However, we define value diversity as concept distinct from alignment. Specifically, we measure the dissimilarity between agents responses and aggregate these differences into system-level diversity score, either through pairwise averaging across all agents or through structural averaging over the agent dissimilarity graph. Under this formulation, diversity is treated as collective property of the system rather than an attribute of any individual agent. Using the World Values Survey (Haerpfer et al., 2022), we conduct extensive experiments across 19 cultures, 18 backbone models, and wide range of system configurations (4). Our main findings are as follows. First, single-backbone systems are systematically less diverse than the human reference. When single LLM serves as the backbone for all agents in multicultural system, none of the 18 singlebackbone systems reaches the human diversity level (best system: 36.12, human: 44.07). Moreover, stronger backbone capability does not naturally translate into higher system-level value diversity (5). Second, diversity reveals system properties that alignment misses. Across all systems, diversity and alignment exhibit almost no correlation, with Pearson = 0.12. Several systems achieve high alignment while remaining highly homogeneous internally. Value diversity therefore complements alignment by capturing system-level properties that per-agent measures cannot reflect (5.1). Third, mixed backbones improve both alignment and diversity. In realistic deployments, different cultural agents may operate on different backbone models due to user preferences. We exhaustively explore all 185 1.89M such configurations and find that the mixed-backbone Pareto frontier consistently dominates the single-backbone frontier along both axes. Nevertheless, the gap to human-level diversity persists (5.2). Fourth, neither culture selection nor agent count rescues diversity. We vary both the cultures populating the system and the number of agents. Neither factor improves system-level diversity. More importantly, as the number of agents increases, the homogenization of multicultural agent systems becomes increasingly amplified (5.3). Fifth, dynamic interaction preserves alignment but reduces diversity. Beyond static systems, we study multi-round social exposure, where agents observe other agents responses before generating their own. We find more complex dynamic than predicted by Social Identity Theory (Tajfel and Turner, 2004): although social exposure slightly improves per-agent cultural fidelity, agents drift more toward consensus, thereby reducing the systems collective plurality. Additional rounds of interaction do not recover the lost diversity (6). Finally, in democratic decision-making setting based on Participatory Budgeting, we find that highdiversity systems produce broader societal-priority coverage and greater plurality in public-resource allocation than low-diversity systems (7). Together, these findings establish value diversity as distinct and currently unmet challenge for the increasingly popular paradigm of multicultural multi-agent systems.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Cultural Alignment of LLMs. Existing cultural alignment evaluation falls into two strands. One focuses on cultural values, with most works using social-value surveys such as WVS (Haerpfer et al., 2022) or Hofstede (Hofstede, 1984) to measure how closely model aligns with specific culture (Cao et al., 2023; AlKhamissi et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2024b; Masoud et al., 2025; Xu et al., 2025). The other focuses on cultural knowledge, measuring how well model reflects culturespecific commonsense and norms (Myung et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2024; Chiu et al., 2025; Singh et al., 2025). Both are evaluated at the per-model level, whereas we shift the focus to the system level. Multi-Agent LLM Systems. Most multi-agent LLM systems are capability-oriented, improving task performance through agent debate and collaboration (Du et al., 2024; Liang et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2025). second line is social-simulationoriented, studying the dynamic behaviors that emerge among agent societies (Feng et al., 2026; 2 Manik and Wang, 2026; Lin et al., 2026; Jiang et al., 2026). This direction has recently gained momentum, exemplified by Moltbook (Moltbook, 2026), an agent-native social network where each agent is initialized with distinct user identity. However, how to evaluate the cultural behavior of such systems remains an open question; we address this gap from the perspective of value diversity.

sections.title: 3.1 System Definition
sections.content: We abstract agent-driven social platforms such as MoltBook (Moltbook, 2026) into multicultural agent system. To capture cultural plurality in its minimal form, we formalize such systems as follows. We consider multi-agent system = {a1, . . . , aN }, where each agent ai is assigned cultural identity ci through its system prompt. The system thus contains culturally grounded agents. Prompt details in this section are provided in Appendix A.1.

sections.title: 3.2 Answering the World Values Survey
sections.content: After system initialization, each agent ai answers value-related survey questions under its assigned cultural identity ci. We instantiate this process on the World Values Survey (WVS) (Haerpfer et al., 2022), which contains value-related multiplechoice questions. For each question, the options are ordinally organized according to the degree of support for the underlying viewpoint. Each agent ai answers all questions and produces response vector x(i) RK, where each entry corresponds to the ordinal index of the selected response option.

sections.title: 3.3 Value Alignment
sections.content: Prior work evaluates how closely model responses align with target culture. Since WVS additionally provides population-level responses for each culture, we follow prior work (Wang et al., 2024b; Xu et al., 2025) and use the majority-vote answer as the cultural ground-truth vector µ, representing the prototypical value orientation of that culture. The alignment of agent ai to culture ci is defined as Align(x, µ) = 1 (cid:113)(cid:80)K k=1(xk µk)2 (cid:113)(cid:80)K k=1 2 , (1) where denotes the maximum possible disagreement on question (e.g., = 3 for 4-point 3 Likert item). Overall alignment is then defined as Alignment(S) =

sections.title: 3.4 Our Metric: Value Diversity
sections.content: Alignment evaluates each agent against its own cultural reference but ignores the relationships among agents within the system. To address this limitation, we introduce system-level value diversity. Alignment and diversity capture conceptually different properties: alignment measures agent-to-human similarity, whereas diversity measures agent-toagent dissimilarity. The two can therefore vary independently. For example, system whose agents collapse toward culturally averaged responses may still achieve high alignment while exhibiting low diversity. Pairwise Diversity We first define Pairwise Diversity, which measures the average dissimilarity across all agent pairs. Given two agents with response vectors and y, their pairwise dissimilarity is defined as Div(x, y) = (cid:113)(cid:80)K k=1(xk yk)2 (cid:113)(cid:80)K k=1 2 , (3) and the pairwise diversity of the system is DiversityP (S) = 1 (cid:0)N 2 (cid:1) (cid:88) i<j Div(cid:0)x(i), x(j)(cid:1). (4) Structural Diversity DiversityP (S) averages all (cid:0)N (cid:1) pairwise distances, but many of these 2 distances are geometrically redundant and contribute limited additional information about the systems global spread. We therefore complement DiversityP (S) with Structural Diversity, which averages only the 1 distances required to connect all agents through the minimum spanning tree (MST) of the pairwise distance graph: DiversityS(S) =

Pseudocode implementations of both metrics are provided in Appendix A.2. For the human reference, we replace agent responses with the corresponding majority-vote WVS response vectors for each culture and apply the same diversity metrics.

sections.title: Model
sections.content: gpt-5.4 gpt-5-mini gpt-4o-mini

sections.title: 4 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Survey We use Wave 7 of the World Values Survey (WVS) (Haerpfer et al., 2022), which contains 260 value-related questions and collects human responses from 57 countries between 2017 and 2020. From the original 260 items, we retain 223 and exclude 37 questions targeting daily-life specifics rather than general value orientations. Cultures From the 57 countries in WVS Wave 7, we select 19 spanning multiple continents: AUS, BOL, BRA, CAN, CHN, DEU, ETH, GBR, IND, KEN, MEX, NGA, NLD, NZL, RUS, THA, UKR, USA, and ZWE. Full country names and corresponding cultural identities are provided in Appendix B. Backbone Models We use 18 LLMs spanning the GPT, Claude, Gemini, Grok, Qwen, and Llama families as backbones, as shown in Table 1. We use default sampling parameters for API-based models (temperature = 1.0, top-p = 1.0) and temperature = 0.6 for the Qwen and Llama series. System Configuration Our main experiments use = 5 agents per system, instantiated with BRA, CHN, MEX, NGA, and NZLfive cultures chosen for their substantial real-world differences. The remaining cultures are reserved for further analysis (5.3). We study two backbone settings: (i) single, where all agents in system share single backbone model, and (ii) mixed, where different agents use different backbones.

sections.title: 5 Main Diversity Results
sections.content: Table 1 reports DiversityP and DiversityS for single-backbone systemsall five cultural agents (BRA, CHN, MEX, NGA, NZL) sharing single backbonealongside the human reference. All systems fall substantially below the human reference on both metrics. The most diverse system, gemini-2.5-pro, reaches 36.12 / 29.60 versus 44.07 / 39.37 for humansgaps of roughly 8 and 10 points on [0, 100] scale. The larger gap under DiversityS further suggests that Structural Diversity provides sharper characterization of systemlevel diversity by discounting redundant inter-agent Release Date DiversityP / Mar 2026 Aug 2025 Jul 2024 Apr 2026 Sep 2025 Oct 2024 Mar 2026 Dec 2025 Jun 2025 Apr 2026 Jul 2025 Feb Feb 2026 Apr 2025 Sep 2024 Apr 2025 Dec 2024 Jul 2024 25.06 / 19.54 31.43 / 25.39 25.46 / 19.59 28.44 / 22.36 26.57 / 21.25 28.85 / 22.65 32.55 / 27.24 33.21 / 26.95 36.12 / 29. 27.42 / 23.93 30.60 / 24.66 25.18 / 19.82 29.82 / 23.25 24.61 / 18.97 20.44 / 16.19 23.60 / 19.33 33.21 / 27.00 34.98 / 29.03 44.07 / 39.37 claude-opus-4.7 claude-sonnet-4.5 claude-3.5-haiku gemini-3.1-flash-lite gemini-3-flash-preview gemini-2.5-pro grok-4.3 grok-4 grokQwen3.5-27B Qwen3-32B Qwen2.5-32B-Instruct llama-4-scout llama-3.3-70b-instruct llama-3.1-70b-instruct Human Table 1: System-level value diversity of multicultural agent systems in the single-backbone setting. Backbones are grouped by model family. Bold marks the highest LLM score per metric and the human baseline. Abbreviation of gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview. relations, pattern consistently observed across other backbones. Moreover, more recent backbones within model family rarely produce the most diverse system. For example, gpt-5.4 exhibits lower diversity than both older GPT-family backbones. This suggests that general model capability does not naturally translate into higher cultural value diversity at the system level. Overall, these findings demonstrate the role of value diversity in revealing system-level cultural homogenization. In the following sections, we further examine its relationship with alignment (5.1) and investigate additional factors shaping diversity in multicultural agent systems, including mixed backbones (5.2), cultural composition, and agent count (5.3).

sections.title: Misses
sections.content: We define value diversity as distinct from alignment, but their relationship remains unclear. Prior work has reported tensions between alignment and diversity in synthetic LLM populations (Murthy et al., 2025). We examine this relationship in multicultural agent systems. We measure Alignment for the 18 single-backbone systems and jointly vi4 Figure 1: Landscape of system-level value diversity and value alignment. Left: 18 single-backbone systems on the (Diversity, Alignment) plane, colored by model family. Dashed lines indicate across-system means. Pearson correlation between the two metrics is reported. Right: Per-question (Dq, Aq) distributions for the two circled systems; bubble size encodes item density, and stars mark across-question means. sualize it with DiversityP in Figure 1 (left).1 Across all systems, the Pearson correlation between diversity and alignment is only = 0.12, indicating no strong relationship between them. However, perfectly human-aligned system would reproduce the human diversity level of 44.07, suggesting that some positive relationship should exist in principle. The absence of this pattern here further highlights limitations of current LLMbased agent systems in capturing finer diversity alignment dynamics of real human societies. Importantly, the weak correlation shows that value diversity captures information not reflected by alignment alone. system can achieve high overall alignment while still exhibiting substantial internal homogenization, as illustrated by grok-3. Conversely, system with relatively high value diversity may still fail to align well with human, as represented by gemini-2.5-pro. We next analyze these two representative cases to better understand the relationship between diversity and alignment. distance among all agents, while Aq measures the mean agent-to-human response similarity; both are normalized to [0, 1]. We observe that grok-3 concentrates more heavily in the upper-left quadrant, indicating that its agents tend to remain close to the corresponding cultural ground truth (aligned) while still converging toward similar responses (homogeneous). In contrast, gemini-2.5-pro shifts more heavily toward the lower-right quadrant, suggesting that its agents generate more diverse but less humanaligned responses. Taken together, these findings show that value diversity captures system properties that alignment alone cannot reflect. Knowing how well system aligns with target cultures is insufficient to determine whether it also preserves their value plurality. This establishes value diversity as complementary evaluation axis for multicultural agent systems.

sections.title: 5.2 Mixed Backbones
sections.content: Two Complementary Cases To illustrate these complementary patterns, Figure 1 (right) decomposes the overall (Diversity, Alignment) results of the grok-3 and gemini-2.5-pro systems into per-question (Dq, Aq) distributions. For each question q, Dq measures the mean pairwise response 1Appendix reports DiversityS with similar findings. In the remainder of the paper, we primarily report DiversityP for readability and interpretability. In realistic deployments, different agents may run on different backbones due to user preferences. We ask how such mixed-backbone systems compare with their single-backbone counterparts. With = 5 cultural slots and 18 candidate backbones, the configuration space contains 185 1.89M assignments, of which 18 are single-backbone. We exhaustively evaluate all of them and visualize the resulting diversity-alignment landscape in Figure 2. 5 Figure 2: Diversityalignment landscape of all 185 1.89M backbone configurations (N = 5 cultures). Blue hexbin shows configuration density (darker = more). Three notable mixedbackbone configurations (green stars) are annotated with D(Diversity), A(Alignment) relative to the nearest single-backbone reference (circled). The mixed-backbone Pareto frontier (solid green) strictly dominates the single-backbone one (dashed orange) across the entire diversityalignment plane. Three configurations illustrate different benefits from the mixed-backbone frontier. At the alignment-optimal end, mixed backbones improve alignment by = +1.51 over the best single-backbone reference; at the diversity-optimal end, they improve diversity by = +1.65. Between the two extremes, balanced configuration improves on comparable single-backbone reference along both axes simultaneously (D = +3.18, = +1.21). These results suggest that mixed backbones have the potential to jointly improve system-level diversity and alignment.

sections.title: Agent Count
sections.content: Two additional factors shape multicultural agent system: which cultures populate it, and how many agents it contains. Here, we investigate how these two factors affect system-level diversity. We select the three most advanced gpt-5.4, claude-opus-4.7, and backbones: gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview, and fix each as the single backbone of system. For each, we (i) fix = 5 and exhaustively evaluate all systems built from (cid:0)19 (cid:1) = 11,628 culture subsets, and (ii) 5 vary the agent count from 2 to 19, reporting the 6 Figure 3: Effects of culture selection and agent count on system-level value diversity. (a) Diversity of all (cid:1) = 11,628 five-culture subsets, sorted ascending. (cid:0)19 5 (b) System-to-human diversity gap as the agent count varies. For each k, we report the maximum diversity over all (cid:0)19 (cid:1) culture subsets for both the system and the human reference. maximum diversity over all (cid:0)19 (cid:1) subsets at each k. Culture Composition (Figure 3a) Even the highest-diversity subset reaches only 29.235.5 across the three backbones. Most configurations cluster within the middle range (2030), indicating that nearly any five-culture sample yields similar moderate diversity. Agent Count (Figure 3b) To account for the natural reduction in average pairwise distance as the number of agents increases, we report the relative difference from the human reference for each agent count k.2 As shown, although the difference is relatively small for small k, the disparity between LLM-based systems and human societies becomes increasingly pronounced as grows. Overall, these results suggest that culture selection alone provides limited gains in system-level value diversity. As the number of agents increases, the initial homogenization of multicultural agent systems becomes increasingly amplified. 2Inter-agent distances may shrink as grows simply because the response space becomes more densely populated; comparing against the human reference at the same controls for this baseline effect. Figure 4: Effect of one-round social exposure on system-level diversity and alignment. Each value is the change relative to the static system of Section 5. All systems lose diversity (D < 0), while alignment generally rises but by much smaller margin.

sections.title: 6 Towards Dynamic Interaction
sections.content: Our experiments so far focus on static systems, where each agent answers the WVS independently. Real-world agent-driven platforms, however, are inherently interactive. We ground our interaction experiments in Social Identity Theory (Tajfel and Turner, 2004), which suggests that exposure to outgroup members can strengthen in-group identification and sharpen between-group distinctiveness. We thus ask whether dynamic interaction increases system-level diversity by reinforcing each agents culturally distinctive positions.

sections.title: 6.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: We study dynamic interaction through multi-round social exposure. In each round, before answering WVS item, each agent is shown the previous-round responses of the other 1 agents to the same item and then produces its own answer. We iterate this process for rounds, where round 0 corresponds to the static system in Section 5. We report systemlevel value diversity and alignment after each round of exposure and compare the results against the static baseline. We adopt the same configuration as the main experiments: five cultural agents (N = 5) with cultures BRA, CHN, MEX, NGA, and NZL sharing single backbone model. Prompt details are provided in Appendix D.1. Figure 5: System diversity over five rounds of interaction for six representative systems. an average decrease of = 1.27. Alignment, in contrast, generally increases by much smaller margin. These findings suggest more complex dynamic than predicted by Social Identity Theory. Although social exposure slightly improves peragent cultural fidelity, agents do not respond by reinforcing culturally distinctive positions. Instead, agents tend to drift toward consensus, thereby reducing the systems collective plurality. Multi-round interaction does not recover diversity. Figure 5 shows the evolution of system diversity under multi-round interaction.3 After > 1 rounds of exposure, some systems, such as claude-opus-4.7, maintain the diversity level reached after the first interaction round. Many other systems, however, continue to lose diversity, albeit with moderate fluctuations. Crucially, no system recovers toward its round-0 diversity baseline. These results suggest that homogenization induced by social exposure represents lasting tendency of interactive multicultural agent systems, rather than merely transient effect. Overall, these findings suggest that the static diversity measured in the main experiments may overestimate the diversity these systems would exhibit in realistic interactive deployments.

sections.title: 7 How Diversity Shapes Collective
sections.content: Decision-Making

sections.title: 6.2 Results
sections.content: Diversity decreases under social exposure. We conduct experiments on representative backbones across six model families. Figure 4 shows the results after one round of social exposure. Every system loses diversity under social exposure, with We next investigate how system-level value diversity affects collective decision-making behavior. More specifically, we ask whether systems with low and high value diversity exhibit different be3The corresponding alignment dynamics are reported in Appendix D.2. 7 Figure 6: Collective decision-making outcomes in the Participatory Budgeting task. Both systems use claude-opus-4.7 as the backbone model. The results show that the high-diversity system distributes support across substantially broader societal dimensions. havioral patterns when making group decisions.

sections.title: 7.1 Experimental Setup
sections.content: Participatory Budgeting To study this question, we consider democratic decision-making scenario based on Participatory Budgeting (PB), where citizens directly propose and vote on urban projects to determine how limited public resources should be allocated (Yang et al., 2024). Rather than using projects from specific municipality, we directly construct the candidate pool using the 13 value dimensions in the WVS dataset, each corresponding to socially beneficial public project.4 Decision-Making After initializing system consisting of culturally grounded agents, each agent independently votes for 4 projects out of the 13 candidates. To obtain statistically stable behavioral patterns, we sample each agent 20 times, resulting in 4 20 project approvals in total. We then compute the aggregated vote-frequency distribution over all projects. By comparing highand low-diversity systems, we analyze how value diversity shapes collective societal prioritization. Agent System We use claude-opus-4.7 as the backbone. The number of agents in system is fixed to = 5. Based on the exhaustive culturalcomposition analysis in Section 5.3, we select the lowest-diversity and highest-diversity systems. We conduct the participatory budgeting task independently for each system, resulting in vote-frequency distributions over 400 project approvals per system. 4The project categories cover Culture, Security, Transparency, Economy, Technology, Civic Participation, Institutions, Environment, Community, Education, Social Inclusion, Migration, and Health.

sections.title: We conduct
sections.content: the same experiment using gpt-5.4 and gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview, both of which exhibit trends consistent with the claude-opus-4.7 results. Full project descriptions, prompts, and additional backbone results are provided in the appendix E.

sections.title: 7.2 Results
sections.content: Figure 6 compares the aggregated vote-frequency distributions produced by lowand high-diversity systems. The low-diversity system exhibits highly concentrated collective decision-making behavior, with approvals collapsing onto only few societal In contrast, the high-diversity sysdimensions. tem produces substantially broader societal coverage. This case study suggests that value diversity is not merely an intrinsic property of multicultural multi-agent system, but may also shape downstream collective behavior. Here, more diverse systems exhibit broader societal-priority coverage and greater plurality in public-resource allocation.

sections.title: 8 Conclusion
sections.content: We propose value diversity as system-level evaluation axis for multicultural multi-agent systems, complementing the per-agent value alignment paradigm. Across 19 cultures, 18 backbones, and millions of system configurations, we find empirically that diversity complements alignment as distinct evaluation axis. All single-backbone systems fall below the human diversity reference, and although mixed backbones narrow this gap, substantial differences from human societies persist across culture selection and agent scaling. Social interaction further erodes diversity by driving 8 agents toward consensus, and participatory budgeting case study shows that this homogenization directly narrows collective decision-making. Together, our results establish value diversity as distinct and currently unmet challenge for increasingly realistic LLM-based societies.

sections.title: Limitations
sections.content: Simplified Interaction and Decision-Making Settings Our interaction and collective decisionmaking experiments adopt simplified settings, including social exposure and WVS-grounded participatory budgeting. The lowand high-diversity configurations in the participatory budgeting study additionally differ in cultural composition as well as measured diversity, which our current design does not fully disentangle. More realistic agent interactions, social networks, and real-world policy environments remain important directions for future work. Cultural Prototype as Reference Our framework compares culturally conditioned agent responses against WVS population majority votes, treating both as prototypical value orientations. While our prompts explicitly elicit typical cultural responses, single LLM sample remains an imperfect approximation of the underlying cultural prototype, whereas WVS majority votes aggregate over large human populations. This asymmetry may affect the absolute comparability between human and model diversity scores. Moreover, WVS-7 responses were collected between 2017 and 2020, and our scores should be interpreted relative to this temporal snapshot rather than timeless cultural reference. Generalizability Our framework adopts deliberately minimal abstraction of multicultural multiagent systems, with the goal of remaining applicable to more complex settings. Whether the homogenization patterns reported here generalize to richer cultural signalssuch as everyday dialogue, normative reasoning, and emergent behaviors on agent-native platformsremains an open question, and we leave instantiations of our framework in such settings to future work.

CoRR, abs/2601.19921. System-Level Value Diversity Details A.1 Agent Prompt After system initialization, each agent answers WVS questions under its assigned cultural identity. For an agent ai with culture ci, we use the following system prompt: You are respondent from {country}. typical Answer cultural values in {culture} culture. questions based on For each WVS question, the user prompt is: Question: {question} Output boxed{number} format MUST be exactly: Discussion Importantly, the prompt is intentionally minimal. We do not provide detailed demographic profiles, behavioral descriptions, or culturespecific stereotypes beyond the target cultural identity itself. This design follows prior survey-based cultural alignment work (Wang et al., 2024b; Xu et al., 2025) and aims only to activate the models internal representation of the corresponding culture. As result, the measured alignment and diversity primarily reflect the backbone models intrinsic cultural knowledge and value associations, rather than effects introduced by complex prompt engineering. A.

sections.title: Implementation of Diversity Metrics
sections.content: Algorithm 1 and Algorithm 2 summarize the implementations of Pairwise Diversity and Structural Diversity used throughout the paper. Structural Diversity is implemented through minimum spanning tree (MST) extraction using the SciPy graph library5. 5https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/ 11 Algorithm 1 Pairwise Diversity Implementation Require: Agent response dictionary all_answers Require: WVS question metadata ref_dict 1: agents sorted agent list 2: pair_dict {} 3: pairwise_scores [ ] 4: for each agent pair (ai, aj) do vec1 all_answers[ai] 5: vec2 all_answers[aj] 6: common_qs shared answered questions 7: squared_sum 0 8: max_squared_sum 0 9: for each question common_qs do 10: 11: 12: 13: 14: 15: 16: 17: 18: end if delta number of options for minus 1 if delta = 0 then continue end if Accumulate: if / ref_dict then continue squared_sum += (x(i) x(j) ) 19: Accumulate: Algorithm 2 Structural Diversity Implementation Require: Pairwise distance dictionary pair_dict Require: Agent list agents 1: agents 2: if < 2 or pair_dict is empty then 3: 4: end if 5: Initialize symmetric distance matrix: return 0 RN 6: for each agent pair (ai, aj) do Retrieve pairwise distance: 7: 8: Fill: dij pair_dict(ai, aj) [i, j] dij [j, i] dij 9: end for 10: Compute minimum spanning tree: MST(M ) max_squared_sum += delta2 11: Compute: 20: 21: 22: 23: 24: end for if max_squared_sum = 0 then continue end if Compute normalized distance: mst_span = (cid:80) (i,j)T [i, j] 1 12: Return: DiversityS(S) = mst_span dij = squared_sum max_squared_sum Append dij to pairwise_scores Store dij in pair_dict 25: 26: 27: end for 28: Return: DiversityP (S) = mean(pairwise_scores)

sections.title: B Country Details in Experiments
sections.content: Table 2 lists the country codes, full country names, and corresponding cultural identities used throughout the experiments.

sections.title: C Additional Results on
sections.content: DiversityAlignment Relationship the Figure 7 reports relationship between Alignment and DiversityS (Structural Diversity). Similar to the pairwise metric in the main paper, the correlation between the two remains weak, further supporting the conclusion that alignment does not reliably reflect whether system preserves generated/scipy.sparse.csgraph.minimum_spanning_ tree.html internal cultural plurality. Compared with DiversityP , DiversityS produces sharper separation between systems with genuine structural plurality and those whose diversity mainly arises from redundant pairwise disagreement. In particular, several highly aligned systems exhibit noticeably lower structural diversity, suggesting that their agents converge toward structurally homogeneous value configurations despite maintaining reasonable alignment with cultural references.

sections.title: You may consider these answers before
sections.content: making your decision. Output format MUST be exactly: boxed{number} Figure 7: Relationship between Structural Diversity and Alignment. Each point represents one singlebackbone multicultural agent system. Dashed lines indicate across-system means. Figure 8: System alignment over five rounds of interaction for the six representative systems. D.2 Multi-turn Alignment Dynamics Collective Decision-Making Details Figure 8 shows the corresponding alignment dynamics for the same six representative systems. In contrast to the consistent diversity loss observed in the main paper, alignment exhibits no systematic trend across rounds. Most systems fluctuate within narrow band around their round-0 value, and no system shows sustained gain or loss comparable in magnitude to the diversity drop. This asymmetry reinforces our main finding: social exposure primarily reshapes the systems collective plurality rather than its per-agent cultural fidelity. In other words, homogenization under interaction is system-level phenomenon that alignment-based evaluation alone would fail to detect. 13 Project Descriptions Table 3 lists the 13 projects used in the participatory budgeting task. Each project is derived from corresponding value dimension in the WVS dataset. The project names and descriptions were generated using GPT-5.5 to transform abstract value dimensions into concrete public-policy initiatives suitable for participatory budgeting scenarios. sections.title: Health
sections.content: Happiness and Wellbeing Funding community festivals and educational programs that preserve traditional religious customs, ceremonies, and local cultural heritage. Expanding AI-assisted public surveillance cameras and emergency response systems to improve urban safety and crime prevention. Creating an open digital platform that allows citizens to track government spending, procurement, and public project budgets. Supporting local manufacturing, industrial infrastructure, and employment growth through public investment programs. Establishing community AI labs, robotics education centers, and startup incubation programs for emerging technologies. Funding local civic centers that organize public discussions, citizen assemblies, and participatory decision-making activities. Supporting public education programs on national institutions, governance systems, civic responsibility, and political leadership. Investing in renewable energy, carbon reduction projects, urban sustainability programs, and environmental protection initiatives. Supporting local volunteer organizations, neighborhood associations, and public community-building activities. Funding educational campaigns promoting honesty, social responsibility, anti-corruption awareness, and ethical public behavior. Supporting programs that promote social inclusion, reduce discrimination, and encourage understanding across different social groups. Providing language education, job assistance, and community integration services for immigrants and refugees. Expanding public mental health support, community wellness activities, and accessible counseling services. Table 3: Public projects used in the WVS-grounded participatory budgeting task. sections.content: is: national participatory budgeting program is allocating limited public funding development initiatives. societal across There are 13 candidate projects: [Project category, description) list WVS (ID, project dimension, for omitted name, and brevity] Voting instruction: Select EXACTLY should receive funding. 4 projects that 14 Base Consider on your long-term choices societal priorities and values. cultural preferences, governance social development, and public well-being. priorities, Output format MUST be exactly: boxed{id1, id2, id3, id4} For example: boxed{2, 5, 8, 11} Additional Results Additional results using gpt-5.4 and gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview are shown in Figure 9 and Figure 10, respectively. Both backbones exhibit trends consistent with the claude-opus-4.7 results presented in the main paper. Figure 9: Collective decision-making outcomes in the Participatory Budgeting task. Both systems use gpt-5.4 as the backbone model. The results show that the high-diversity system distributes support across substantially broader societal dimensions. Figure 10: Collective decision-making outcomes in the Participatory Budgeting task. Both systems use gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview as the backbone model.
